subject: Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. (2021) and student off-campus / social-media speech
central_question: You post something on a weekend, off school grounds, from your own phone — can your school still punish you for it?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands how a 14-year-old's frustrated Snapchat reached the Supreme Court in 2021, and where a school's power over what you say now ends in the age of the phone.
surprise: A student got suspended for a weekend post made off campus, on her own phone — and the Supreme Court said the school went too far, while refusing to say schools can NEVER reach off-campus speech.
stakes: Whether a public school can discipline students for what they say online, after hours, away from school — a question that now touches almost every student and parent.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience: students, parents, and anyone interested in free speech and how systems work
demand_evidence: 'Can your school punish you for a social-media post?' is a live, high-relevance question for every student and parent in the smartphone era.
Recent (2021), recognizable 'cheerleader Snapchat case', advertiser-safe, evergreen.
competition: Coverage is mostly news/legal; few give a neutral 'where school authority ends online' explainer tied to a free-speech series.
differentiation: Present-tense, self-relevant (your phone, your weekend); recent and recognizable; opens the 'what you can SAY' cluster after the EP6-10 'what the state can see/take' arc.
research_feasibility: High: the opinion is public domain (594 U.S. 180); Cornell LII, Oyez and the ACLU document the facts.
visual_feasibility: High: a phone screen with a disappearing post; a 'schoolhouse gate' motif; an after-hours bedroom vs. a classroom; a map of where 'school' begins and ends.
risk_notes: Decided landmark case (2021), 8-1; opinion is public domain - advertiser-friendly.

Brandi Levy (B.L.) was a minor at the time but went public with the ACLU after the ruling — safe to name, describe neutrally and sympathetically-but-factually; no real-person likeness in AI visuals (invariant 11).
ADVERTISER SAFETY: the post's caption contained profanity — DESCRIBE it ('a profane, frustrated caption'); the narrator must NOT read the explicit words. On-screen text must be censored.
Keep neutral on the school-vs-student question; present the school's interest (order, the cheer team) fairly alongside the free-speech side.
kill_conditions: Cannot source the holding and key facts to the primary opinion plus one independent reference.
